revision.text:
[[de:Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITLE = Lunar Calendar |NAME = M4:X1-W4:Z2-N35-N10:N35:W3
Renpet-hebu-en-heb-en-pesdjenetiu
"Rnpt-ḥbw-n-ḥb-psḏntjw"
"Year of celebrations of the [[:en:New Moon|New Moon]] festival" |NAME-EXTENDED = [[Ancient Empire (Egypt|Ancient Empire]] |BILD1 = Lunar libration with phase Oct 2007.gif |IMAGE1-WIDTH =250px |BILD1-DESCRIPTION = Animation of the [[moon phase]]n }} {{complex|qq=}} The '''{{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ancient_Egyptian_Lunar_Calendar|en|draft:Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|en:draft|Lunar calendar (Ancient Egypt)|de|de:Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qid=Q1944246|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar}}''' in {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|qid=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egypt|1=Ancient Egypt}}, i.e., those among the simultaneously common {{ill|Egyptian calendar |en|Egyptian calendar |qid=Q254101|lt= Egyptian calendars|1=Egyptian calendar}}, whose beginning of the month was always defined by the same moon phase, were [[ Bound lunar calendar]] in all documented cases. The beginning of a calendar year should preferably be the beginning of the [[:en:Nile flood|Nile flood]]. It was associated very early with the appearance of the {{ill|Sirius|en|Sirius|qid=Q3409|lt=Sothis|1=Sirius}} in the night sky. Therefore, the oldest documented calendars of Egypt also choose this event to determine the beginning of the year. This is also the case with the "Sothis lunar calendar". The beginning of the month in Egypt was always considered to be the beginning of the non-visibility of the moon before sunrise, a month ended after the [[Old light]]. Thus, the Egyptian lunar calendars differed from those of many other cultures, in which a new month usually began with the new light. ===Lunar calendar=== [[File:2017 Lunar Calendar.png|thumb|right|200px|A modern lunar calendar for 2017]] The Egyptians appear to have used a purely [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunar calendar]] prior to the establishment of the solar civil calendar{{sfnp|Parker|1950|pp=30-32|qq=}} {{sfn|Høyrup|p=13|qq=}} in which each month began on the morning when the {{ill|waning moon|en|waning moon|qid=Q34604447|lt=waning|1=Waning moon}} crescent moon could no longer be seen.{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=23|qq=}} Until the closing of Egypt's {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|qid=Q9163|lt=polytheist|1=Polytheism}} temples under the {{ill|Byzantine Egypt|en|Byzantine Egypt|qid=Q17302295|lt=Byzantines|1=Byzantine Egypt}}, the lunar calendar continued to be used as the {{ill|Liturgical year|en|Liturgical year|qid=Q51617|lt=Liturgical year|1=Liturgical year}} of various cults.{{sfn|Høyrup|p=13|qq=}} The lunar calendar divided the month into four weeks, reflecting each quarter of the {{ill|Lunar phase|en|Lunar phase|qid=Q26388|lt=Lunar phase|1=Lunar phase}}s.{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA3 3–4]|qq=}} Because the exact time of morning considered to begin the Egyptian day remains uncertain{{harvp|Schaefer|2000|p=[http://articles.adsabs.harvard.edu/full/gif/2000JHA....31..149S/0000153.000.html 153]–[http://articles.adsabs.harvard.edu/full/gif/2000JHA....31..149S/0000154.000.html 154]|qq=}}. and there is no evidence that any method other than observation was used to determine the beginnings of the lunar months prior to the {{nowrap|4th century BC,{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=29|qq=}}|qq=}} there is no sure way to reconstruct exact dates in the lunar calendar from its known dates. The difference between beginning the day at the first light of dawn or at sunrise accounts for an 11–14 year shift in dated observations of the lunar cycle.{{sfnp|O'Mara|2003|p=18|qq=}} It remains unknown how the Egyptians dealt with obscurement by clouds when they occurred and the best current algorithms have been shown to differ from actual observation of the waning crescent moon in about one-in-five cases. {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|en|Richard Anthony Parker|qid=Q2148992|lt=Parker|1=Richard Anthony Parker}} and others have argued for its development into an observational and then calculated {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|qid=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}{{sfnp|Parker|1950|pp=13-29|qq=}} which used a 30 day {{ill|Intercalation (timekeeping)|en|Intercalation (timekeeping)|qid=Q1225911|lt=intercalary|1=Intercalation (timekeeping)}} month every two to three years to accommodate the [[:en:lunar year|lunar year]]'s loss of about 11 days a year relative to the [[:en:Solar year|Solar year]] and to maintain the placement of the [[heliacal rising]] of [[:en:Sirius (star)|Sirius]] within its {{ill|Mesori|en|Mesori|qid=Q1621298|lt=twelfth month|1=Mesori}}.{{sfnp|Parker|1950|pp=30–32|qq=}} No evidence for such a month, however, exists in the present historical record.{{sfnp|Tetley|2014|p=[http://www.egyptchronology.com/uploads/2/6/9/4/26943741/ch_10_resolving_the_eponymous_month_conflict.pdf 153]|qq=}} {{hiero|{{nowrap|Temple Month|qq=}}
{{nowrap|''Ꜣbd n ḥwt-nṯr''{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=17|qq=}}|qq=}}|N11:N14-N35-R8-O6|align=left|qq=}} A second lunar calendar is attested by a {{ill|Demotic (Egyptian)|en|Demotic (Egyptian)|qid=Q188519|lt=demotic|1=Demotic (Egyptian)}} astronomical papyrus{{cite book|url=http://pcarlsberg.ku.dk|title=The Papyrus Carlsberg Collection|publisher=University of Copenhagen|location=Copenhagen, DK|contribution=Papyrus Carlsberg 9|access-date=11 February 2017|qq=}} dating to sometime after 144 AD which outlines a [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|Lunisolar]] calendar operating in accordance with the Egyptian civil calendar according to a 25 year cycle.{{sfnp|Parker|1950|pp=13–23|qq=}} The calendar seems to show its month beginning with the first visibility of the waxing crescent moon, but {{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|en|Richard Anthony Parker|qid=Q2148992|lt=Parker|1=Richard Anthony Parker}} displayed an error in the cycle of about a day in 500 years,{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA25 25]|qq=}} using it to show the cycle was developed to correspond with the new moon around 357{{nbsp|qq=}}BC.{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA26 26]|qq=}} This date places it prior to the [[:en:Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic period]] and within the native Egyptian [[:en:Dynasty XXX|Dynasty XXX]]. Egypt's {{ill|Twenty-seventh Dynasty of Egypt|en|Twenty-seventh Dynasty of Egypt|qid=Q926624|lt=1st Persian occupation|1=Twenty-seventh Dynasty of Egypt}}, however, seems likely to have been its inspiration.{{sfn|Høyrup|p=14|qq=}} This lunisolar calendar's calculations apparently continued to be used without correction into the {{ill|Roman Egypt|en|Roman Egypt|qid=Q202311|lt=Roman period|1=Roman Egypt}}, even when they no longer precisely matched the observable lunar phases.{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=27|qq=}} The days of the lunar month — known to the Egyptians as a "temple month"{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=17|qq=}} — were individually named and celebrated as stages in the life of the moon god, variously [[:en:Thoth (god)|Thoth]] in the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|qid=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom|1=Middle Kingdom of Egypt}} or [[:en:Khonsu (god)|Khonsu]] in the [[:en:Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic era]]: "He ... is conceived ... on ''Psḏntyw''; he is born on ''Ꜣbd''; he grows old after ''Smdt''".{{sfnp|Parker|1950|pp=11–12|qq=}} {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |+ '''Days of the lunar month'''{{sfnp|Parker|1950|pp=11–12|qq=}} {{efn|For further variations, see [[:en:Heinrich Brugsch|Brugsch]].{{cite book |last=Brugsch |first=Heinrich |author-link=Heinrich Brugsch |title=Thesaurus Inscriptionum Aegyptiacarum |location=Leipzig, DE |date=1883 |pages=46–48 |qq=}}.|qq=}} |- ! rowspan="2" | Day ! colspan="3" | Name |- ! colspan="2" | Egyptian ! Meaning (if known) |- | align="center" | 1 | align="center" | N10-G4-W3{{efn|Variant representations of the day of the new moon include N10:N35-G4-W3, N10:N35-W3,{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=11|qq=}} N9-G4-W3,{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=1231|qq=}} N9:N35-G4-W3:N5, N9:N35-G4-X4, N9:N35-W3, N9:N35-W3:N5, N9:N35-X1-G4-W3, N9:N35-X1:Z4-G4-W3:N5, N9:N35-X1:Z5-W3:N5,{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=1232|qq=}} Q3:O34-D46:N35-N10,{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=1668|qq=}} D1:Z1-N11:N14-W3, and D1:Z1-M6-X1:Z1;{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=33|qq=}} D12*X1:N35-G4-W3,{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=12|qq=}} and Z2:Z2:Z2-W3:N35 in the Middle Kingdom; and Z2:Z2:Z2-W24:X1-G4-W3:N5 in later inscriptions.{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=13|qq=}}|qq=}} || ''Psḏtyw''{{efn|In later sources, ''Psḏntyw''.{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=11|qq=}}|qq=}} || Literal meaning unknown but possibly related to the [[:en:Ennead (gods)|Ennead]]; the day of the [[:en:New Moon|New Moon]]. |- | align="center" | 2 | align="center" | D1-N11:N14{{efn|Variant representations of the day of the first crescent moon include N11:N14, N11:N14-D46:W3,{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=11|qq=}} D1:Z1-N11:N14-W3,{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=33|qq=}} D1-N11-N5 (properly N11A with the moon turned 90° clockwise),{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=27|qq=}} and D1:Q3-M17-M17-M17-G1-D46:X1-N5-Z1:Z1:Z1.{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=28|qq=}}|qq=}} || ''Tp Ꜣbd''
''Ꜣbd'' || "Beginning the Month" or "The Month"; the beginning of the {{ill|Lunar phase|en|Lunar phase|qid=Q26388|lt=Crescent Moon|1=Lunar phase}}. |- | align="center" | 3 | align="center" | F31-Q3:D21-W3 || ''Mspr'' || "Arrival" |- | align="center" | 4 | align="center" | O1:D21-X1-S29-G17-W3 || ''Prt Sm'' || "The Going Forth of the ''Sm''", a kind of priest |- | align="center" | 5 | align="center" | Aa1:X1-D2:Z1-R2-W3 || ''I͗ḫt Ḥr Ḫꜣwt'' || "Offerings upon the Altar" |- | align="center" | 6 | align="center" | S29-T22-N35:X1-Z2:Z2-W3{{efn|Variant representations of the 6th day of the lunar month include Z2:Z2-N35:X1-W3,{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=12|qq=}} S29-T22-N35:X1-Z2:Z2-X2*W22:X6, S29-T22-N35:X1-Z2:Z2-X2*W22:Z8,{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=1885|qq=}} T22-N35:X1-X2*W22:X4-Z1:Z1:Z1,{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=1997|qq=}} Z2:Z2:N35-X1:W3, Z2:Z2:N35:X1-W4, and Z1-Z1-Z1-Z1-Z1-Z1-N35:X1-W4.{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=2464|qq=}}|qq=}} || ''Snt'' || "The Sixth" |- | align="center" | 7 | align="center" | D46:N35-M17-X1-W3{{efn|Variant representations of the 1st-quarter day include D46:N35-M17-X1:V11-W3:N5 and D46:N35-M17-X1-W3:N5.{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=277|qq=}}|qq=}} || ''Dnı͗t'' || "Partial"; the first-quarter day. |- | align="center" | 8 | align="center" | D1*D12:W3 || ''Tp'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 9 | align="center" | F19-Q3:W3{{efn|Properly, the first sign is not an animal jawbone F19 but the rarer, similar-looking figure of a lion's forepaw F118B.{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=11|qq=}}|qq=}} || ''Kꜣp'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 10 | align="center" | S29-M17-I9:D52-W3 || ''Sı͗f'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 11 | align="center" | F29-N8-Z2:W3 || ''Stt'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 12 | align="center" | N31:D53-N31:D53-W3 || Unknown || "Partial" the second-quarter day. |- | align="center" | 13 | align="center" | D12-D12-U1-A59-W3{{efn|Properly, the two circles D12 are shrunk and placed within the curve of the sickle U1, forming U43.{{sfnp|Everson|1999|p=57|qq=}} The male figure should be man sowing seeds A60, which includes a curve of dots coming from the man's hand.{{sfnp|Everson|1999|p=5|qq=}}|qq=}} || ''Mꜣꜣ Sṯy'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 14 | align="center" | S32-G1-Z7-W3 || ''Sı͗ꜣw'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 15 | align="center" | D1-N13{{efn|Variant representations of the day of the full moon include N13-X1:W3, N13-V20:Z1*Z1*Z1*Z1*Z1-N35:X1-W3,{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=11|qq=}} D1-N13-W3, D1-N33:V20-Z1:Z9, {{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=27|qq=}} N13, and N13-N35:X1-W3.{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=1235|qq=}}|qq=}} || ''Smdt''
''Tp Smdt'' || Literal meaning uncertain; the day of the [[:en:Full Moon|Full Moon]]. |- | align="center" | 16 | align="center" | F31-Q3:D21-Z1*Z1:W24-W3 || ''Mspr Sn Nw''
''Ḥbs Tp''{{sfnp|Parker|1950|p=18|qq=}} || "Second Arrival"
"Covering the Head" |- | align="center" | 17 | align="center" | S32-G1-Z7-W3 || ''Sı͗ꜣw'' || Second Quarter Day |- | align="center" | 18 | align="center" | M17-V28-N12-W3{{efn|Properly, N12\t1 or N12A, with the crescent moon N12 turned 90° clockwise.|qq=}} || ''I͗ꜥḥ'' || "Day of the Moon" |- | align="center" | 19 | align="center" | F21-S43-S43-S43-I9:W3 || ''Sḏm Mdwf'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 20 | align="center" | U21:Q3-W3 || ''Stp'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 21 | align="center" | Aa20-D21:G43-W3{{efn|Variant representations of the 21st day of the lunar month include Aa20-D21-G43-W3 and Aa20-D21:W3.{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=917|qq=}}|qq=}} || ''Ꜥprw'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 22 | align="center" | F22-M44-X1:W3 || ''Pḥ Spdt'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 23 | align="center" | D46:N35-M17-X1:V11-W3 || ''Dnı͗t'' || "Partial"; the third-quarter day. |- | align="center" | 24 | align="center" | V31:N35-V28-G43-N2-W3{{efn|Variant representations of the 24th day of the lunar month include V31:N35-V28-G43-N2.{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=2294|qq=}}|qq=}} || ''Knḥw'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 25 | align="center" | F29-N8-Z2:W3 || ''Stt'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 26 | align="center" | O1:D21-X1:W3 || ''Prt'' || "The Going Forth" |- | align="center" | 27 | align="center" | G43-N37-D58-W3{{efn|Variant representations of the 27th day of the lunar month include Z7-D310-W3.{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=2472|qq=}} D310 is a foot D58 crossed by a variant of pool N37 with 2{{sfnp|Everson|1999|p=25|qq=}} or 3{{sfnp|Vygus|2015|p=2472|qq=}} diagonal strokes across it.|qq=}} || ''Wšb'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 28 | align="center" | O23-W24*X1:N1-W3 || ''Ḥb Sd Nwt'' || "The [[:en:Jubilee (ancient Egypt)|Jubilee]] of {{ill|Nut (goddess)|en|Nut (goddess)|qid=Q165496|lt=Nut|1=Nut (goddess)}}" |- | align="center" | 29 | align="center" | P6-A47-W3 || ''Ꜥḥꜥ'' || Unknown |- | align="center" | 30 | align="center" | O1:D21-X1:D54-O34:R12:X1*Z4-W3{{efn|Properly, the loaf X1 and diagonal strokes Z4 are shrunk and fit under the two sides of the standard R12.|qq=}} || ''Prt Mn'' || "The Going Forth of {{ill|Min (god)|en|Min (god)|qid=Q192322|lt=Min|1=Min (god)}}" |- |}
{| ! colspan="2" |The moon as God |- | class="hiero" |Aa1:N35:O34-M23-G43 |[[:en:Chons|Chons]]
"ḪNsw" |- ! colspan="2" |Sothis as goddess |- | class="hiero" |S29-M44-Q3:D46-X1:N14 |{{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|qid=Q930167|lt=Sopdet|1=Sopdet}}
"Spd.t" |- ! colspan="2" |The year (also a goddess) |- | class="hiero" |rnp:t |Renpet
"rnp:t" |} == Fundamentals of the calculation of time == General consensus is that the concepts of day and (lunar) month were developed before that of the year. A [[day (Ancient Egypt)|day]] always began with sunrise in Ancient Egypt. Thus, the events underlying the daily and monthly count were always before the day or month defined by this: * The night when the moon became a [[:en:New Moon|New Moon]] before sunrise was the last in [[#The month|month]]. * The [[:en:heliacal|heliacal]]e rise of Sirius always took place at the end of the year. The New Year began after that with the beginning of the month. The events usually fell in the 12th hour of the night, whereby each night, just like the [[:en: light day| light day]], always had exactly 12 hours. The length of the hours changed accordingly with the seasons. Every hour, efforts were made to keep the course of the sun going in all the sun sanctuaries with ritual praises, but at least not to let a certain, processually conceived context of meaning be torn off. This context probably lies in the regularity. In [[#d-Assmann 2005|Assmann 2005]] it is then also stated: {{Quote|text=Unlike in Mesopotamia and other divination cultures, the attention directed to the cosmos was not directed to the exceptions, but to the rules. In the cyclical regularity of his processes, the divinity of the cosmos was revealed to the Egyptian.|source=[[#d-Assmann 2005|Assmann 2005]], page 113.|qq=}} So it is not surprising that, for example, the 15th day was always counted as the full moon day, regardless of the astronomical accuracy. == Variants of the lunar calendar == Since the beginning of the Egyptian calendar, the lunar calendar has also been further developed. === The original lunar calendar === The oldest documented mentions of lunar calendar dates are found from about 2350B.C. in the {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|qid=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts|1=Pyramid Texts}}n. However, the use of {{ill|Astronomy|en|Astronomy|qid=Q333|lt=astronomical|1=Astronomy}} records is already documented under King [[Wadji]] around 2880 B.C.
{| style="width:210px;" ! colspan="2" | /Opener of the year |- | class="hiero" |F14 also F15 / |[[Wepet-renpet]]
"Wpt-rnp.door |- ! colspan="2" |New Year |- | class="hiero" |T8-rnp-t:Z1-X4|/"tpy rnp:t"|- ! colspan="2" |Sothis and Wepet-renpet |- | colspan="2" class="hiero" |R8-M44-N14:X1*H8-Z9*X1*H8:V30-F13-M4 |- | colspan="2" |"Sothis the Great,
Mistress of Wepet renpet." |- | colspan="2" style="text-align: center;" | {{Annotated image | image = Sirius A and B Hubble photo.jpg | image-width = 210 | image-left = 0 | image-top = -50 | width = 210 | height = 150 | noframe = 1 | outer-css = margin:0;|qq=}} Sirius, the signaler for the lunar New Year in the Sothis lunar calendar |}
The original lunar calendar must have been based solely on observations. In any case, these observations included those of the [[:en:heliacal|heliacal]]en rise of the Sothis. This event was called the "opener of the Year". This name was then also used for the 12th month in the "'Sothis year"', even if the event took place only after that. The first month of the New year then began after this event with the sunrise, before which the Moon was not visible again for the first time. This results in a leap year regulation based on observations, in which a 13th month was switched on in each case. In [[#d-Parker 1950|Parker 1950]], the assumption was made that this happened exactly when the following month began less than 12 days after the Wepet-renpet event. In this case, the month that began after Wepet-renpet would be an intercalary 13. Month. With such a definition, he would have ensured that the event always fell in the month of the same name. However, evidence that the beginning of a month has been calculated has not yet been found. As a result, however, an attempt was made by a number of scientists to conclude on the basis of {{ill|Astronomy |en|Astronomy |qid=Q333|lt= astronomical|1=Astronomy}} calculations on exact calendar dates in the {{ill|proleptic|en|proleptic|qid=Q2112660|lt=proleptic|1=Proleptic}} en {{ill|Julian calendar |en|Julian calendar |qid=Q11184|lt= Julian Calendar|1=Julian calendar}}. But in fact, this is not possible. Instead, errors in lunar observation must be assumed: * Algorithms for calculating the visibility of the [[old light]]thumb rules followed and proved to be incorrect in the majority of cases. Even better, more recent predictions are wrong in about 20%, that is, in 2-3 out of 12-13 cases. * Clouds could prevent the recognizability of the new moon. * Other sources of error may stand in the way of an estimation of the historical sighting. Although Bradley E. Schaefer also states that "the currently great uncertainties in the prediction of lunar visibility do not allow a possible astronomical solution of absolute Egyptian chronology with lunar data", this does not prevent other scientists from continuing their research in this direction. In the meantime, the optical density and suspected historical dust pollution of the air are taken into account in the estimation of the visibility of the rise of Sirius. In addition, the fact that the position of the lunar months in the {{ill|Sidereal year|en|Sidereal year|qid=Q2281928|lt=Sidereal year|1=Sidereal year}} fluctuates due to the length of the month and the repeated activation of an [[activation (time calculation) |intercalary]], thirteenth month stands in the way of general, narrowly limited information about the position of the months of the lunar calendar relative to our {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}}. Nevertheless, a rough estimate can be given: {| class="zebra center" |- ! colspan="5" |Seasonal location of the Egyptian months compared to Central Europe |- ! colspan="4" style="border-bottom:none;" |Month name (lunar calendar) ! rowspan="2" |season
(Central Europe) |- |} ==S ee Also== * {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Day|de|Tag (Altes Ägypten)|en:draft|Ancient Egyptian Day|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egyptian Day|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egyptian Day|qid=Q1669420|1=Ancient Egyptian Day}} ==Notes== ===Notelist=== {{notelist|qq=}} ===References=== {{reflist|qq=}} === Bibliography === * {{citation |last=Clagett |first=Marshall |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |date=1995 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |location=Philadelphia |publisher=American Philosophical Society |isbn=9780871692146 |author-link=Marshall Clagett|qq=}}. * {{citation |last=Everson |first=Michael |title=Encoding Egyptian Hieroglyphs in Plane 1 of the UCS |date=1999 |url=https://www.unicode.org/L2/L1999/N1944.pdf |publisher=Unicode|qq=}}. * {{citation |last=Forisek |first=Péter |title=Censorinus and His Work ''De Die Natali'' |date=2003 |url=https://dea.lib.unideb.hu/dea/bitstream/handle/2437/79618/tz_en894.pdf?sequence=3 |location=Debrecen |publisher=University of Debrecen|qq=}}. ([https://dea.lib.unideb.hu/dea/bitstream/handle/2437/79618/de_894.pdf?sequence=2&isAllowed=y Full Hungarian version].) * {{citation |last=Grafton |first=Anthony Thomas |title=The Classical Quarterly, ''Vol. XXXV, No. 2'' |date=1985 |pages=454–465 |contribution=Technical Chronology and Astrological History in Varro, Censorinus, and Others |ref={{harvid|Grafton & al.|1985|qq=}} |display-authors=1 |author2=Noel Mark Swedlow |author-link=Anthony Grafton|qq=}}. * {{citation |last=Høyrup |first=Jens |title=Physis |contribution=A Historian's History of Ancient Egyptian Science |contribution-url=http://webhotel4.ruc.dk/~jensh/Publications/1996%7BR%7D10_Clagett.PDF|qq=}}, a review of Clagett's ''Ancient Egyptian Science'', Vols. I & II. * {{citation |last=Jauhiainen |first=Heidi |title=Do Not Celebrate Your Feast without Your Neighbors: A Study of References to Feasts and Festivals of Non-Literary Documents from Ramesside Period Deir el-Medina |date=2009 |url=https://helda.helsinki.fi/bitstream/handle/10138/19196/donotcel.pdf?sequence=2 |series=''Publications of the Institute for Asian and African Studies'', No. 10 |location=Helsinki |publisher=University of Helsinki|qq=}}. * {{citation |title=Ancient Egyptian Chronology |date=2006 |editor-last=Krauss |editor-first=Rolf |series=''Handbook of Oriental Studies'', Sect. 1, Vol. 83 |location=Leiden |publisher=Brill |ref={{harvid|''AEC''|2006|qq=}} |editor2=Erik Hornung |editor3=David A. Warburton |editor4=Marianne Eaton-Krauss |display-editors=1|qq=}}. * {{citation |last=Luft |first=Ulrich |title=Egypt and the Levant, ''Vol. XVI'' |date=2006 |pages=309–316 |contribution=Absolute Chronology in Egypt in the First Quarter of the Second Millennium BC |publisher=Austrian Academy of Sciences Press|qq=}}. * {{citation |last=Neugebauer |first=Otto Eduard |title=Acta Orientalia, ''No. 16'' |date=1939 |pages=169 ff |contribution=Die Bedeutungslosigkeit der 'Sothisperiode' für die Älteste Ägyptische Chronologie |author-link=Otto E. Neugebauer|qq=}}. {{in lang|de|qq=}} * {{citation |last=O'Mara |first=Patrick F. |title=Journal of Near Eastern Studies, ''Vol. LXII, No. 1'' |date=January 2003 |pages=17–26 |contribution=Censorinus, the Sothic Cycle, and Calendar Year One in Ancient Egypt: The Epistemological Problem |location=Chicago |publisher=University of Chicago Press|qq=}}. * {{citation |last=Parker |first=Richard Anthony |title=The Calendars of Ancient Egypt |date=1950 |url=https://oi.uchicago.edu/sites/oi.uchicago.edu/files/uploads/shared/docs/saoc26.pdf |series=''Studies in Ancient Oriental Civilization'', No. 26 |location=Chicago |publisher=University of Chicago Press |author-link=Richard Anthony Parker|qq=}}. * {{citation |last=Schaefer |first=Bradley Elliott |title=Journal for the History of Astronomy, ''Vol. XXXI, Pt. 2'' |date=2000 |volume=31 |pages=[http://articles.adsabs.harvard.edu//full/2000JHA....31..149S/0000149.000.html 149–155] |contribution=The Heliacal Rise of Sirius and Ancient Egyptian Chronology |contribution-url=http://articles.adsabs.harvard.edu//full/2000JHA....31..149S/0000149.000.html |bibcode=2000JHA....31..149S |doi=10.1177/002182860003100204 |s2cid=118433565 |author-link=Bradley Schaefer|qq=}}. * {{citation |last=Spalinger |first=Anthony |title=Journal of Near Eastern Studies, ''Vol. 54, No. 1'' |date=January 1995 |pages=33–47 |contribution=Some Remarks on the Epagomenal Days in Ancient Egypt|qq=}}. * {{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings, ''Vol. I'' |date=2014 |url=http://www.egyptchronology.com/vols-1--2.html |access-date=2017-02-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170211075421/http://www.egyptchronology.com/vols-1--2.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=2017-02-11|qq=}}. * {{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |date=1940 |pages=447–464 |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock|qq=}}. * {{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |date=2015 |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf|qq=}}. == External links == * [http://www.instonebrewer.com/TyndaleSites/Egypt/ptolemies/chron/egyptian/chron_eg_intro.htm Detailed information about the Egyptian calendars, including lunar cycles] * [http://egypt.online-resourcen.de/content/Date-Converter-Ancient-Egypt Date Converter for Ancient Egypt] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20050216102054/http://emr.cs.iit.edu/home/reingold/calendar-book/Calendrica.html Calendrica] Includes the Egyptian civil calendar with years in {{ill|Ptolemy|en|Ptolemy|qid=Q34943|lt=Ptolemy|1=Ptolemy}}'s Nabonassar Era (year 1 = 747 BC) as well as the Coptic, Ethiopic, and French calendars. * [http://www.egyptologyforum.org/civil4.0.zip Civil, ver. 4.0], is a 25kB DOS program to convert dates in the Egyptian civil calendar to the Julian or Gregorian ones {{Authority control|qq=}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect
